name: The mosque and the grave of the Prophet ﷺ
epithet: The most honored resting place on earth

era.anchor: His house, and the centuries after

title: The mosque and the grave of the Prophet ﷺ: the most honored resting place on earth
meta_description: Day 102 of the seerah: the simple house of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, his washing and burial, the grave in Aisha's chamber, the mosque built over it across the centuries, and the adab of visiting, retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's landmark series.
keywords: seerah, Prophet Muhammad, grave of the Prophet, house of the Prophet, Masjid an-Nabawi, green dome, Aisha chamber, Yasir Qadhi seerah, adab of visiting, ziyarah, burial of the Prophet

video.title: Seerah of Prophet Muhammad 102 - The Mosque & Grave of Muhammad ~ Dr. Yasir Qadhi | 4th Feb 2015

source_note: Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 102: the mosque and the grave of the Prophet ﷺ (Memphis Islamic Center, 2015). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: There is no spot on this earth more honored than the patch of ground we are about to stand beside. It is a grave, and it is a home, and for fourteen centuries it has been the place a believer's heart turns toward after the Kaaba itself. Today, near the very end of our hundred-day walk with him ﷺ, Dr. Yasir Qadhi does something he has never quite done before: he takes us inside the house, shows us the bed, and then tells us what happened to that bed, and to the body laid to rest in it, across the long centuries that followed.

It is a strange and beautiful way to say goodbye. Not with a battle or a sermon, but with a room: ten feet by twelve, a roof of palm leaves that let the rain in, and inside it the most beloved human being who ever lived.

sections.h2: A house you could touch the walls of

ayah.arabic: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِن وَرَاءِ الْحُجُرَاتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ
ayah.text: Indeed, those who call you, [O Muḥammad], from behind the chambers, most of them do not use reason.

paras: When the Prophet ﷺ came to Madinah, he built his mosque, and against its back wall he built homes for his family: small chambers of unbaked clay, one for Sawda, one for Aisha, and as the years passed, more for the rest of the mothers of the believers, with the house he gave his daughter Fatima and Ali close beside them. When the qibla was later turned, the houses that had stood behind the mosque now stood in front of it. Only Aisha's chamber opened directly into the mosque itself, its doorway shared with the house of Allah.

Do not picture anything grand. The Sheikh asks you to measure it honestly: the room was about three by three and a half meters, ten feet by twelve. That is the size of a rug you would roll out in a living room, and inside that single room was the sleeping, the cooking, the storing, the whole of a life. The roof was woven palm leaves, never sealed, so the rain came through and the bitter desert cold came through, and the man Allah had honored above all creation was not sheltered from either. A solid roof was only built over those houses after he ﷺ had died.

Around the chambers there seems to have been a low clay wall, a barrier for the privacy of his wives, and it is to this that Allah points when He speaks of those who would rudely call out to the Prophet ﷺ from behind the apartments. The wall let him move from one wife's house to the next without stepping out among strangers. There is even a gentle dispute among scholars, the Sheikh notes, over whether a narrow alleyway ran between Aisha's wall and the mosque or whether the two walls were one. He calls it what it is: a trivial question, pleasant to wonder about, impossible to settle, and Allah knows best.

The bed was a mattress stuffed with the fibers of date palm, resting on a frame of stripped branches, set against the wall that the mosque shared. There was no kitchen, no second room, no plumbing; a small chamber pot served at night, and for the rest they walked out beyond the city every two or three days, eating so little that they needed no more. This is the house the Sheikh wants burned into your memory before he tells you what came next.

When the Prophet ﷺ passed away, the companions were unsure where to lay him, until Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu remembered that the Prophet ﷺ had told him every prophet is buried where he dies. So his bed was lifted, and they dug his grave in that very spot, in the floor of Aisha's chamber. They dug it as a lahd: a shaft down, then a niche cut into the side toward the qibla, the body laid into the niche, the opening sealed with bricks so that no earth would fall upon him. The majority of scholars hold the lahd to be the sunnah, and one of their proofs is simply this, that Allah would have chosen only the best for His Prophet ﷺ. He was not undressed; over his garments they placed three white cloths, and they laid him down.

sections.h2: The woman who lived beside the graves
paras: Aisha radiyallahu anha went on living in her own house, with the grave of her husband ﷺ resting in one part of it. When her father Abu Bakr died, he asked to be buried beside his companion, and she gave the space gladly; now there were two graves in her home, and she lived on beside them for years.

Then Omar radiyallahu anhu was stabbed, and knowing he was dying, he sent his son Abdullah with a message framed with breathtaking humility: tell Aisha that Omar, not the caliph, but a man named Omar, asks permission to be buried with his two companions. If she hesitates at all, said Omar, leave, and do not press her; it is her house and her right. She had been keeping that last space for herself, and she gave it away without a pause: I will prefer him over my own self. So a third grave was opened in her room, and now Aisha hung a curtain and lived in the small space that remained.

Sit with what the Sheikh lingers on here, because it moved him visibly. For roughly twelve years this woman ate and slept and prayed in a single room while, just on the other side of a curtain, lay the graves of her husband the Messenger of Allah ﷺ and her own father. When she herself was dying, far later, in the time of Muawiyah, she asked not to be buried there but among the other wives of the Prophet ﷺ in al-Baqi. Perhaps out of deference to Omar, perhaps because she felt it more fitting to rest with the women; we are not told, and the Sheikh does not guess for her.

For ninety years the houses simply stood there, empty after the wives had passed, tended by the family of the Prophet ﷺ. Then the caliph al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik resolved to expand the mosque in all four directions, and that meant absorbing the chambers of the mothers of the believers into it. Why he insisted on this, the Sheikh weighs carefully and refuses to flatten. One reading is that he was simply a ruler doing what rulers do, leaving his mark with a magnificent mosque and caring little for what stood in the way. The other reading, which the Sheikh says he finds the more persuasive, is political: the family of the Prophet ﷺ was deeply loved, and Ali ibn Husayn, that gentle survivor of Karbala, would sit and even teach in his ancestor's house, which opened onto the mosque. To a nervous throne, the simplest answer was to remove the house. The Sheikh names this plainly as the corrupting pull of power, and asks Allah to protect us all from ever sitting in such a seat.

A great scholar of Madinah objected, Saeed ibn al-Musayyib among them. But notice precisely why, because the Sheikh corrects a common misreading: Saeed did not object on some theological ground about graves inside a mosque. He objected because he wanted the people to keep seeing the rough, bare simplicity of the Prophet's ﷺ houses, so that their hearts would stay soft. Within ninety years, civilization had already come to Madinah: two-story homes, fine fabric, money flowing in from conquered empires. Saeed wanted a living reminder of how the Messenger of Allah ﷺ had actually lived, and the ruler did not care to keep it.

So the chambers were demolished, all but Aisha's. Around her grave-chamber they raised a solid structure of thick black blocks, the same stone the Kaaba is built from, with no door at all. And as they worked, a workman cried out in fright: two feet were protruding from the wall, fresh after ninety years, and they feared these were the feet of the Prophet ﷺ himself. A scholar of the family was called, and he reassured them: these are the feet of our grandfather Omar. They covered them and built on.

sections.h2: The pentagon, the thefts, and the fires
paras: It was Omar ibn Abd al-Aziz, then the young governor of Madinah, who oversaw the building, and the structure he raised around the chamber was a pentagon, five-sided on purpose. The scholars give two reasons: so that no one would face it in prayer as though it were a qibla, and so that no newcomer to Islam would mistake a black cube in Madinah for a second Kaaba. That pentagon would stand for some five hundred years, and to this day no one has entered the innermost chamber it guards.

The books then carry stories of attempts to steal the body of the Prophet ﷺ. The most famous tells of two men posing as devout worshippers near the chamber, secretly digging a tunnel toward it, until the ruler Nur al-Din Zangi, warned in a dream, rode to Madinah, found them out, and poured molten lead around the grave to seal it. The Sheikh tells the story and then, honestly, sets it down: he has researched it and cannot believe it. The earliest reference appears more than two centuries after the supposed event, and a man responsible for the sanctuary today told him plainly there is no such lead barrier around the grave. He treats it as a later invention to glorify a beloved figure, and Allah knows best. Two fires, however, are certain. In 654 after the Hijra a lamp-keeper's oil caught a curtain and the whole mosque burned, the very year Baghdad fell to the Mongols, so no answer ever came from a caliph who was about to lose his life. The mosque was rebuilt around the chamber out of sheer reverence, no one daring to disturb what had fallen inside. A second great fire came in 886, when lightning struck the minaret on a stormy night and the mosque burned again.

sections.h2: The green dome and the grille
paras: Out of those rebuildings came the shapes a visitor sees today. After the first fire, the sultan Baybars raised a square wall around the chamber and, for the first time in the history of Islam, a dome over the grave; there had never been one before. After the second fire, the great scholar al-Samhudi was sent into the inner chambers to clean and restore them, the first human in some five centuries to enter, and the last for the centuries since. He wrote of it in trembling first-person: he stepped only to the rear threshold, would go no further out of awe, and caught a fragrance unlike anything he had ever known. He stood as long as he could, sending salam upon the best of the prophets ﷺ and his two companions, and made all the dua he could. He found the graves settled almost flat, a little reddish gravel upon them, the earth still moist as if freshly dug. From his time the wooden enclosure became the metal lattice, the green grille, that pilgrims see to this day, six hundred years old.

And the dome itself: Baybars's wooden dome was rebuilt in solid form by later sultans, and for most of its life it was not green at all. It was bare metal, then blue, then white. Only around 1837, in the reign of Sultan Mahmud, was it painted green for the first time. The famous green dome of Madinah, the Sheikh reminds us, is barely a century and a half old, and there is no religious meaning to the color itself. A curtain hung around the grave, a custom begun in early Abbasid times and never stopped, and the cloth that covers it is woven in the same Makkah workshop that weaves the cover of the Kaaba, changed once in ten years.

sections.h2: Where we stand and give salam
paras: So when you finally come, and may Allah let every one of us come, here is what is real and what is imagined. From outside the grille you give your salam: peace to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, then to Abu Bakr beside him, then to Omar. You stand perhaps ten feet from him ﷺ. Through the lattice you will see only the curtain; there is no house to see, no graves to glimpse. Anyone who claims to have seen more, the Sheikh says firmly, is imagining it. No one enters the innermost chamber. There are no keys to it, and no one should ever try.

Keep the adab the companions kept. The body of the Prophet ﷺ was left exactly where he died, out of the utmost respect, and that reverence is the inheritance every generation has guarded, through fire and flood and the schemes of kings. You do not need to touch a wall or peer through a grille to be near him. You were near him from the first day of this journey, every time his name crossed your lips and you sent your salawat after it.

We began this seerah, a hundred days ago, with a question: who is he ﷺ? We end it standing quietly outside a small room in Madinah, having walked his whole life, and the answer is no longer a list of his specialities. It is love. So send your salam the way al-Samhudi sent his, the way his ummah has always sent it, and carry him with you out of these hundred days and into the rest of your life.

dua.translit: Allahumma salli wa sallim ala nabiyyina Muhammad, wa balligh ruhahu minna tahiyyatan wa salama
dua.en: O Allah, send Your praise and peace upon our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, and convey to his soul our greeting and our peace.

lessons_intro: The last room of the seerah is also one of its plainest, and it leaves you with a handful of things to keep. These run straight out of the Sheikh's closing night.

lessons.bold: Measure your wants against his room.
lessons.text: Ten feet by twelve, a roof that let the rain in, and inside it the most honored soul in creation. When your own appetite for more grows loud, the Sheikh says, remember the house Saeed ibn al-Musayyib begged them not to tear down.

lessons.bold: Generosity can cost you your own resting place.
lessons.text: Aisha kept the last grave in her room for herself, then gave it to Omar the moment he asked. The thing you were saving for yourself is sometimes exactly what you were meant to give away.

lessons.bold: Guard the truth even when the story is beautiful.
lessons.text: The Sheikh loves the Prophet ﷺ, and still he sets down the famous tale of molten lead and the dream, because it does not hold up. Reverence is never an excuse to repeat what is not sound.

lessons.bold: The grave needs no decoration to be honored.
lessons.text: There was no dome for centuries, and the green is barely a century and a half old. What honors that chamber is not its color but the One buried beside it ﷺ, and the salam the ummah has never stopped sending.
You do not need to reach Madinah to be close to the Prophet ﷺ. A hundred days you have walked beside him; the nearness is in the salawat, and that you can send from anywhere on earth.

faq.q: Why is the grave of the Prophet ﷺ inside Masjid an-Nabawi?
faq.a: It was not built that way. The Prophet ﷺ was buried where he died, in the floor of his wife Aisha's house, which stood beside the mosque. About ninety years later the caliph al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik expanded the mosque in every direction and absorbed the chambers, including Aisha's, into the building, so the grave came to sit within the mosque rather than next to it.

faq.q: Who is buried alongside the Prophet ﷺ?
faq.a: Two of his closest companions: Abu Bakr radiyallahu anhu, buried just behind him, and then Omar radiyallahu anhu, who asked Aisha's permission with great humility before he died. A fourth space remains, with some weak narrations suggesting Isa may one day be buried there, but nothing authentic confirms it, so we do not say so for certain.

faq.q: Is the famous green dome an ancient part of the grave?
faq.a: No. There was no dome over the grave at all for the first six centuries of Islam. The first dome was built in the time of Baybars after a great fire, and the dome was painted bare metal, blue, then white over the centuries. It was only painted green around 1837, in the reign of Sultan Mahmud, so the green dome is roughly a century and a half old and the color carries no religious meaning.

faq.q: Did someone really try to steal the body of the Prophet ﷺ?
faq.a: The books of history carry several such stories, the most famous involving two disguised plotters digging toward the chamber and the ruler Nur al-Din Zangi stopping them after a dream and sealing the grave with lead. Dr. Yasir Qadhi tells the story but does not accept it: the earliest reference appears over two centuries later, and those responsible for the sanctuary say there is no such lead barrier. He treats it as a later embellishment, and Allah knows best.

faq.q: What is the right way to visit and give salam at the grave?
faq.a: You stand outside the metal grille and give salam to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, then to Abu Bakr, then to Omar. Through the lattice you see only the curtain, never the graves themselves, and no one enters the inner chamber. The adab is reverence and restraint: the body was left exactly where he died out of respect, and that respect is what every visitor is meant to carry.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: A hundred days ago we asked who he ﷺ was, and we answered with his specialities: the most praised soul in creation, mercy sent walking, the first through the gate of Jannah. Today we have our other answer, and it is smaller and somehow larger. He was a man who slept on date-palm fibers in a room the size of a rug, under a roof that let the cold in, and who was laid to rest in the floor of that same room, beside the two friends who loved him most. Greatness and humility were never two things in him; they were one.

The seerah ends not at a tomb but at a threshold, the way al-Samhudi stopped at the door of the chamber and would go no further. We stand there with him, our salam on our lips, knowing we have walked the whole of this life and that the walking does not really end here. So we send it one last time, and we mean it more than we did on day one. O Allah, send Your praise and Your peace upon our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, the most honored of all who ever lived; let us stand one day at that blessed grille and give him our salam in person; gather us under his banner with Abu Bakr and Omar and all who loved him; and convey to his soul, from this ummah that has never stopped saying his name, our greeting and our peace. Ameen.

The most honored resting place on earth is also a home: ten feet by twelve, with a roof that let the rain in.

His house in Madinah was about 10 ft by 12 ft.

This is how the most beloved man who ever lived chose to live ﷺ.

Every prophet is buried where he dies.

So they lifted his bed and dug his grave in the floor of Aisha's room. Beside him, Abu Bakr. Then Omar, who asked her permission with the words: not the caliph, a man named Omar.

For about twelve years, Aisha lived in that one room, a curtain away from the graves of her husband the Messenger of Allah ﷺ and her own father.

She gave away the last grave, the one she had saved for herself, the moment Omar asked.

Ninety years later, a caliph expanded the mosque over the chambers.

A scholar begged him to stop, not over theology, but so people could keep seeing how simply the Prophet ﷺ had really lived. He was overruled.

The famous green dome?

There was no dome for six centuries. It was bare metal, then blue, then white. Painted green only around 1837.

It is barely 150 years old, and the color carries no religious meaning.

The story of molten lead and a dream and thieves stopped at a tunnel?

Dr. Yasir Qadhi tells it, then sets it down. The earliest source is two centuries too late. Reverence is never a reason to repeat what is not sound.

You stand at the grille and give salam: to the Messenger ﷺ, to Abu Bakr, to Omar.

You see only the curtain. You do not need to see more.

A hundred days we walked with him. The nearness was always in the salawat.
social.caption: Day 102 of 104, the finale of the seerah: the house, the grave, and the mosque of the Prophet ﷺ. A room ten feet by twelve, a roof that let the rain in, and beside him in the end his two dearest friends. We end where his ummah's heart turns after the Kaaba itself, with our salam on our lips. Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's landmark seerah series (episode embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A new day of his life ﷺ, every day, at buruja.com. #seerah #ProphetMuhammad #Buruja #YasirQadhi #MasjidanNabawi #islam #salawat

email.subject: The most honored room on earth ﷺ
email.alt_subject: Ten feet by twelve, with a roof that let the rain in
email.preview: Day 102 of 104: the house, the grave, and the mosque of the Prophet ﷺ.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

After a hundred days, we say goodbye the way few would expect: not with a battle or a sermon, but with a room.

The house of the Prophet ﷺ in Madinah was about ten feet by twelve, the size of a rug you would roll out in a living room. Inside it was the sleeping, the cooking, the whole of a life. The roof was woven palm leaves that never sealed, so the rain came through and the desert cold came through, and the man Allah honored above all creation was sheltered from neither.

He was buried where he slept, in the floor of Aisha's chamber, because he had taught that every prophet is buried where he dies. Beside him they laid Abu Bakr, and then Omar, who sent word as he was dying: tell Aisha that a man named Omar, not the caliph, asks her permission. She gave away the last grave, the one she had kept for herself, without a pause.

Then Dr. Yasir Qadhi walks us through the centuries: the chambers absorbed into the growing mosque, the fires, the famous green dome (which is barely 150 years old and was once blue, then white), and a few beloved legends he gently sets down because they do not hold up. Reverence, he reminds us, is never a reason to repeat what is not sound.

And at the end you stand at the grille and give your salam: to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, to Abu Bakr, to Omar. You see only the curtain. You do not need to see more. A hundred days you have walked beside him, and the nearness was always in the salawat.

Read the finale, and watch the Sheikh's full episode, at the link below. And then send your salam, one more time, and mean it more than you did on day one.
email.cta: Read the finale of the seerah
